message:
chore(canvas,showcase): tighten test assertion + route markdown overlay through unified renderer

Two small follow-ups to the `render_excluding` work landed in the
previous commit.

== crates/ftui-extras/src/canvas.rs ==

The `render_excluding` test was reaching into the frame buffer with
two unwraps:

    let rendered = frame.buffer.get(0, 0).unwrap();
    let skipped = frame.buffer.get(1, 0).unwrap();
    assert_eq!(rendered.content.as_char(), Some('\u{28FF}'));
    assert!(skipped.is_empty());

That panics with a confusing line number if `frame.buffer.get(...)`
returns `None` — which would happen if a future change to the frame
sizing or pool arithmetic put the inspected coordinates outside the
buffer. Tighten to:

    assert_eq!(
        frame.buffer.get(0, 0).map(|cell| cell.content.as_char()),
        Some(Some('\u{28FF}'))
    );
    assert!(frame.buffer.get(1, 0).is_some_and(Cell::is_empty));

Equivalent semantics; difference is the failure shape on a
`get(...)` miss. The first assertion fails as
`assertion left==right ... left: None, right: Some(Some('\u{28FF}'))`
(actionable: "the cell isn't there"). The second uses
`is_some_and(Cell::is_empty)` which fails with the standard
`assertion failed: ...` message but cleanly handles both the
"cell missing" and "cell present but not empty" cases as a single
`false`. Either way, the panic at unwrap site disappears.

== crates/ftui-demo-showcase/src/screens/visual_effects.rs ==

The markdown overlay branch in the visual-effects demo was calling
the OLD path:

    if let Some(overlay) = markdown_overlay_area {
        self.render_markdown_overlay_panel(frame, overlay);
    }

`render_markdown_overlay_panel` is the legacy panel-only renderer
(draws the overlay but doesn't coordinate with the canvas painter
underneath). After the `render_excluding` rework, the canvas path
already excludes the overlay's footprint, so re-using the panel-only
renderer would re-paint the (now-uncovered) gap rather than letting
the unified `render_markdown_overlay` orchestrate the painter +
overlay together.

Switch to:

    if markdown_overlay_area.is_some() {
        self.render_markdown_overlay(frame, canvas_area);
    }

`render_markdown_overlay` consumes `canvas_area` and computes the
overlay rectangle internally — it has visibility into the painter's
exclude shape and renders the overlay to land inside the carved-out
region. The `is_some()` gate preserves the "skip when no overlay
configured" condition; only the renderer call shape changes.
Behaviorally identical when an overlay IS configured; previously
the overlay would draw on top of fully-painted canvas, now it draws
into the painter's reserved gap. Either looks the same on screen —
the optimization is the painter does less work to produce that
output.

`markdown_overlay_area` is no longer needed at this site; the inner
computation in `render_markdown_overlay` derives the overlay
rectangle from `canvas_area` + the screen's overlay-config field.
